title: Allow starting (not creating) a podman play kube deployment
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind feature

**Description**

Using `podman play kube` is great, except when you had something started and restart your machine. In this case the pods exist, but are not started. Executing `podman play kube my-stuff.yaml` doesn't work anymore, as the pods already exists. And there is no "just start existing pods" flag.

It is either necessary to start the pods manually, or recreate them.

It would be nice if existing pods would simply be started. Alternatively a flag like `--activate` or `--reuse` would allow the user to enable this behavior.

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. `podman play kube my-stuff.yaml`

2. Reboot machine

3. `podman play kube my-stuff.yaml`

**Describe the results you received:**

```
$ podman play kube develop/services.yaml
Error: develop/services.yaml: error adding pod to state: name "postgresql" is in use: pod already exists
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

```
$ podman play kube develop/services.yaml
[…]
Pod:
b136988026eec50309905bf69ca277187419f41c86d706ea714fd35c9befaed3

26f22c1dfd6090ecfcfc59850b382ba4f53c70a243e46c3d9554debd06d81a19

Pod:
229097b299423b1730b387c2a89a6ba2065da448cabb93e96b9876c3d83f4e87
Container:
ede9320dc847ea69655daffe1339e6f0b3ca94bc5e5aa403118a5e13d3a33f51
```

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

Every time.

**Output of `podman version`:**

comments.body: isn't this the use case for `podman play kube --replace`?

> isn't this the use case for `podman play kube --replace`?

:+1:

So you believe that the pods and containers should lie dormant, and the default for a service that is starting a kubernetes.YAML file should be loaded with --replace? Is that what happens currently?  Should we add this information to the podman-kube-play/up man page?

> Is that what happens currently? 

No, `--replace` defaults to `false`.

> Should we add this information to the podman-kube-play/up man page?

`--replace` is mentioned in the man pages.
But `--replace` says: "Delete and recreate […]", which would mean, that the state is lost, right? While I can stop and start a pod, and it would keeps its state?
That is a difference between a pod created by hand versus a pod created by play kube.

So if pods created by `podman play kube` aren't supposed to be started again, wouldn't it make sense then to imply `--rm` for such pods? In that case a restart of the host would clear out those pods, right?

> That is a difference between a pod created by hand versus a pod created by play kube.

Correct.  `play kube` tries to follow Kubernetes where "restarting" is not possible.  State must be made persistent outside a container/pod, for instance, on volumes.

> So if pods created by `podman play kube` aren't supposed to be started again, wouldn't it make sense then to imply `--rm` for such pods? In that case a restart of the host would clear out those pods, right?

That would change the current and has a potential to break existing workloads which may rely on podman to error out in case of a name conflict.  But users are free to use `--replace` for that. We do the same in the `podman-kube` systemd template that will be shipped with Podman v4.2: https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/contrib/systemd/system/podman-kube%40.service.in#L12

But I think using `--rm` would still cause a name conflict (when starting play kube a second time) just after a reboot, one would have a clean system, instead of lingering pods which cannot be re-activated anyway.

True, it is more of a cleanup scenario now, and `--replace` would handle the situation when starting again.

I will close this issue. Thanks for the explanation.
I think I misinterpreted your `--rm` suggestion, @ctron.  Do you imagine `--rm` to auto-remove the pods once all containers exited?

My idea was they would disappear once the host gets restarted. But you are right, the would also disappear once the pod would exit. In which I would expect it to be restarted according to the podspec definition. Which is a different thing though, I guess.

This is bringing up some interesting ideas on how we should handle pods and podman kube play.

I think we might want to start a design doc on how we handle Pods in different situations.  And then have a meeting to discuss and design the behavior.
Right now you can already use `podman pod {start,stop}` for the pod created by play kube, so if you have to keep the state you can use this.

> This is bringing up some interesting ideas on how we should handle pods and podman kube play.
> 
> I think we might want to start a design doc on how we handle Pods in different situations. And then have a meeting to discuss and design the behavior.

I am glad this issue turned into something useful :)

@rhatdan lets collect some ideas here first.

So far, the only thing that does not work yet is `--rm` which would remove the containers and pods once they're done.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Perhaps this should be discussed at the face-to-face
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
The `--rm` functionality has been implemented with the `--wait` flag which will clean up on receiving SIGTERM or after the pods exited.  So I think we can close the issue.
